narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 63% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from key aspects of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, LSAT score of 27, Year 1 school decile ranking of 2, and Year 3 decile of 3—all of which sit notably below the typical student's averages of 3.21 GPA, 36.7 LSAT, 5.71 Year 1 decile, and 5.53 Year 3 decile. Your upper-middle family income aligns reasonably with averages, while being male and White places you in the majority of applicants, and opting for part-time status is less common than full-time. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which had the biggest push toward predicting failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, then your LSAT score. Next in influence came your family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, part-time choice, and finally gender—all contributing in that order to nudge the prediction toward failure rather than success. Compared to typical students who pass, your lower academic deciles and test scores stood out as the primary drags, making your profile riskier in the model's eyes. While demographics like gender and race had minimal sway here, they still added slightly to the concern. The good news is several of these factors are within your control for future improvement. To boost your bar exam odds, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim above 36, as it's a top influencer and highly changeable. Strengthening your overall GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program could help offset those low deciles, and considering full-time study might align better with successful patterns. With targeted prep, you could shift your profile toward the averages of bar passers and reapply stronger next time—many students turn predictions like this around with effort. Keep pushing; your upper-middle background gives you solid support to make these changes.
